questions.question: What is the primary responsibility of the Service layer in both Spring Boot and Angular?
options: Handling HTTP request parsing and response formatting
Managing database connections and query execution
Containing business logic that can be reused across multiple controllers or components
Rendering templates and managing UI state

questions.question: Why is putting business logic directly in an Angular component a problem?
options: Angular components cannot contain TypeScript methods
The logic can't be reused in other components and can't be tested without a DOM
Components are compiled away and don't exist at runtime
Angular restricts the number of methods a component can have

questions.question: In Spring Boot, what is the correct layer for a @Service annotated class to sit in?
options: Directly between the HTTP request and the database, replacing the repository
Between the controller (HTTP layer) and the repository (data layer)
Above the controller, acting as an API gateway
At the same level as the controller, handling alternative request formats

questions.question: A UserService needs to create a user AND send a welcome email. Which approach follows the services layer pattern?
options: Put both in the UserController alongside the @PostMapping method
Put both operations directly in the UserRepository
UserService orchestrates UserRepository.save() and EmailService.send() — two focused services
Create one massive ServiceManager class that handles all application operations

questions.question: What does it mean for a controller or component to be 'thin'?
options: It has fewer than 100 lines of code
It only handles framework concerns (HTTP parsing / UI events) and delegates everything else to services
It has no constructor injection — all dependencies are static
It uses only template-driven forms, not reactive forms
